mn38:0.2: The Greater Discourse on the Ending of *taṇhā*
mn38:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn38:10.1: “When one is doubtful, *bhikkhus*, does uncertainty arise about ‘This has come to be’?”
mn38:10.3: “When one is doubtful, *bhikkhus*, does uncertainty arise about ‘This has arisen from nutriment’?”
mn38:10.5: “When one is doubtful, *bhikkhus*, does uncertainty arise about ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’?”
mn38:11.1: “When one sees with correct discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is, *bhikkhus*, is the uncertainty about ‘This has come to be’ abandoned?”
mn38:11.3: “When one sees with correct discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is, *bhikkhus*, is the uncertainty about ‘This has arisen from nutriment’ abandoned?”
mn38:11.5: “When one sees with correct discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is, *bhikkhus*, is the uncertainty about ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’ abandoned?”
mn38:12.1: “Then, *bhikkhus*, are you thus free from doubt here regarding ‘This has come to be’?”
mn38:12.3: “Then, *bhikkhus*, are you thus free from doubt here regarding ‘This has arisen from nutriment’?”
mn38:12.5: “Then, *bhikkhus*, are you thus free from doubt here regarding ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’?”
mn38:13.1: “Then, *bhikkhus*, has ‘This has come to be’ been well seen by you with correct discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is?”
mn38:13.3: “Then, *bhikkhus*, has ‘This has arisen from nutriment’ been well seen by you with correct discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is?”
mn38:13.5: “Then, *bhikkhus*, has ‘With the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease’ been well seen by you with correct discernment [*sammappaññāya*] as it truly is?”
mn38:14.1: “If, *bhikkhus*, you were to cling to, play with, treasure, and make your own even this view, so pure and so bright, would you then understand that I have taught the *dhamma* compared to a raft [*kullūpamaṁ dhammaṁ*] for crossing over, not for grasping?”
mn38:14.3: “But if, *bhikkhus*, you were not to cling to, play with, treasure, and make your own even this view, so pure and so bright, would you then understand that I have taught the *dhamma* compared to a raft [*kullūpamaṁ dhammaṁ*] for crossing over, not for grasping?”
mn38:15.1: “There are these four nutriments, *bhikkhus*, for the maintenance of beings that have come to be, or for the support of those seeking a new becoming.
mn38:15.3: Physical food, whether coarse or subtle, contact as the second, mental intention as the third, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as the fourth.
mn38:16.1: And these four nutriments, *bhikkhus*—what is their source, what is their origin, from what are they born, from what do they arise?
mn38:16.10: The dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] has *nāmarūpa* as its source, *nāmarūpa* as its origin, it is born from *nāmarūpa*, it arises from *nāmarūpa*.
mn38:16.11: And this *nāmarūpa*, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it arise?
mn38:16.12: *Nāmarūpa* has consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as its source, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as its origin, it is born from consciousness [*viññāṇa*], it arises from consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn38:16.13: And this consciousness [*viññāṇa*], *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it arise?
mn38:16.14: Consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has *saṅkhāras* as its source, *saṅkhāras* as its origin, it is born from *saṅkhāras*, it arises from *saṅkhāras*.
mn38:16.15: And these *saṅkhāras*, *bhikkhus*—what is their source, what is their origin, from what are they born, from what do they arise?
mn38:16.16: *Saṅkhāras* have blindness [*avijjā*] as their source, blindness [*avijjā*] as their origin, they are born from blindness [*avijjā*], they arise from blindness [*avijjā*].
mn38:16.2: These four nutriments have *taṇhā* as their source, *taṇhā* as their origin, they are born from *taṇhā*, they arise from *taṇhā*.
mn38:16.3: And this *taṇhā*, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it arise?
mn38:16.4: *Taṇhā* has *vedanā* as its source, *vedanā* as its origin, it is born from *vedanā*, it arises from *vedanā*.
mn38:16.5: And this *vedanā*, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it arise?
mn38:16.6: *Vedanā* has contact as its source, contact as its origin, it is born from contact, it arises from contact.
mn38:16.7: And this contact, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it arise?
mn38:16.8: Contact has the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as its source, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as its origin, it is born from the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], it arises from the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].
mn38:16.9: And this dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it arise?
mn38:17.11: with birth as condition, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress come to be.
mn38:17.12: Thus there is the arising of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:17.4: with *nāmarūpa* as condition, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:17.5: with the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, contact;
mn38:18.1: “‘With birth as condition, aging-and-death’: so it was said.
mn38:18.11: “‘With clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] as condition, *bhava*’: so it was said.
mn38:18.12: But is *bhava* conditioned by clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.14: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.16: “‘With *taṇhā* as condition, clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*]’: so it was said. But is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] conditioned by *taṇhā*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.18: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.2: But is aging-and-death conditioned by birth, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.20: “‘With *vedanā* as condition, *taṇhā*’: so it was said.
mn38:18.21: But is *taṇhā* conditioned by *vedanā*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.23: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.25: “‘With contact as condition, *vedanā*’: so it was said.
mn38:18.26: But is *vedanā* conditioned by contact, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.28: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.30: “‘With the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, contact’: so it was said.
mn38:18.31: But is contact conditioned by the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.32: “Conditioned by the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], *bhante*, is contact;
mn38:18.33: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.34: contact is conditioned by the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].”
mn38:18.35: “‘With *nāmarūpa* as condition, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*]’: so it was said.
mn38:18.36: But is the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] conditioned by *nāmarūpa*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.37: “Conditioned by *nāmarūpa*, *bhante*, is the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:18.38: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.39: the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] is conditioned by *nāmarūpa*.”
mn38:18.4: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.40: “‘With consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as condition, *nāmarūpa*’: so it was said.
mn38:18.41: But is *nāmarūpa* conditioned by consciousness [*viññāṇa*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.43: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.45: “‘With *saṅkhāras* as condition, consciousness [*viññāṇa*]’: so it was said.
mn38:18.46: But is consciousness [*viññāṇa*] conditioned by *saṅkhāras*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.48: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.50: “‘With blindness [*avijjā*] as condition, *saṅkhāras*’: so it was said.
mn38:18.51: But are *saṅkhāras* conditioned by blindness [*avijjā*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.53: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:18.6: “‘With *bhava* as condition, birth’: so it was said.
mn38:18.7: But is birth conditioned by *bhava*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:18.9: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:19.14: with birth as condition, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress come to be.
mn38:19.15: Thus there is the arising of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:19.2: So, *bhikkhus*, you too say thus, and I too say thus—
mn38:19.3: when this exists, that comes to be; with the arising of this, that arises, that is:
mn38:19.7: with *nāmarūpa* as condition, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:19.8: with the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as condition, contact;
mn38:2.1: Now at that time, in a *bhikkhu* named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this evil mistaken view had arisen:
mn38:2.2: “As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.”
mn38:20.1: But with the remainderless fading away and cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], there is the cessation of *saṅkhāras*;
mn38:20.10: with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth;
mn38:20.11: with the cessation of birth, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress cease.
mn38:20.12: Thus there is the cessation of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:20.2: with the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*];
mn38:20.3: with the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*;
mn38:20.4: with the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:20.5: with the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], the cessation of contact;
mn38:20.6: with the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*;
mn38:20.7: with the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*;
mn38:20.8: with the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*];
mn38:20.9: with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], the cessation of *bhava*;
mn38:21.1: “‘With the cessation of birth, the cessation of aging-and-death’: so it was said.
mn38:21.10: with the cessation of *bhava*, there is the cessation of birth.”
mn38:21.11: “‘With the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], the cessation of *bhava*’: so it was said.
mn38:21.12: But is the cessation of *bhava* due to the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.13: “With the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], *bhante*, there is the cessation of *bhava*;
mn38:21.14: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.15: with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], there is the cessation of *bhava*.”
mn38:21.16: “‘With the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*]’: so it was said.
mn38:21.17: But is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] due to the cessation of *taṇhā*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.18: “With the cessation of *taṇhā*, *bhante*, there is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*];
mn38:21.19: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.2: But is the cessation of aging-and-death due to the cessation of birth, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.20: with the cessation of *taṇhā*, there is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*].”
mn38:21.21: “‘With the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*’: so it was said.
mn38:21.22: But is the cessation of *taṇhā* due to the cessation of *vedanā*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.23: “With the cessation of *vedanā*, *bhante*, there is the cessation of *taṇhā*;
mn38:21.24: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.25: with the cessation of *vedanā*, there is the cessation of *taṇhā*.”
mn38:21.26: “‘With the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*’: so it was said.
mn38:21.27: But is the cessation of *vedanā* due to the cessation of contact, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.28: “With the cessation of contact, *bhante*, there is the cessation of *vedanā*;
mn38:21.29: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.3: “With the cessation of birth, *bhante*, there is the cessation of aging-and-death;
mn38:21.30: with the cessation of contact, there is the cessation of *vedanā*.”
mn38:21.31: “‘With the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], the cessation of contact’: so it was said.
mn38:21.32: But is the cessation of contact due to the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.33: “With the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], *bhante*, there is the cessation of contact;
mn38:21.34: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.35: with the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], there is the cessation of contact.”
mn38:21.36: “‘With the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*]’: so it was said.
mn38:21.37: But is the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] due to the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.38: “With the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, *bhante*, there is the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:21.39: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.4: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.40: with the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, there is the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].”
mn38:21.41: “‘With the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*’: so it was said.
mn38:21.42: But is the cessation of *nāmarūpa* due to the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.43: “With the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], *bhante*, there is the cessation of *nāmarūpa*;
mn38:21.44: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.45: with the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], there is the cessation of *nāmarūpa*.”
mn38:21.46: “‘With the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*]’: so it was said.
mn38:21.47: But is the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*] due to the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.48: “With the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, *bhante*, there is the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*];
mn38:21.49: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.5: with the cessation of birth, there is the cessation of aging-and-death.”
mn38:21.50: with the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, there is the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn38:21.51: “‘With the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], the cessation of *saṅkhāras*’: so it was said.
mn38:21.52: But is the cessation of *saṅkhāras* due to the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.53: “With the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], *bhante*, there is the cessation of *saṅkhāras*;
mn38:21.54: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:21.55: with the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], there is the cessation of *saṅkhāras*.”
mn38:21.6: “‘With the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth’: so it was said.
mn38:21.7: But is the cessation of birth due to the cessation of *bhava*, *bhikkhus*, or not, and how is it in this case?”
mn38:21.8: “With the cessation of *bhava*, *bhante*, there is the cessation of birth;
mn38:21.9: this is how it is for us here—
mn38:22.10: with the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*;
mn38:22.11: with the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*];
mn38:22.12: with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], the cessation of *bhava*;
mn38:22.13: with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth;
mn38:22.14: with the cessation of birth, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress cease.
mn38:22.15: Thus there is the cessation of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:22.2: So, *bhikkhus*, you too say thus, and I too say thus—
mn38:22.3: when this is not, that does not come to be; with the cessation of this, that ceases, that is:
mn38:22.4: with the cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], the cessation of *saṅkhāras*;
mn38:22.5: with the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*];
mn38:22.6: with the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*;
mn38:22.7: with the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*];
mn38:22.8: with the cessation of the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], the cessation of contact;
mn38:22.9: with the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*;
mn38:23.1: “If you knew and saw in this way, *bhikkhus*, would you run back to the past, thinking:
mn38:23.2: ‘Were we in the past? Were we not in the past? What were we in the past? How were we in the past? Having been what, what were we in the past?’”
mn38:23.4: “If you knew and saw in this way, *bhikkhus*, would you run forward to the future, thinking—
mn38:23.5: ‘Will we be in the future? Will we not be in the future? What will we be in the future? How will we be in the future? Having been what, what will we be in the future?’”
mn38:24.1: “If you knew and saw in this way, *bhikkhus*, would you now be inwardly perplexed about the present:
mn38:24.10: “If you knew and saw in this way, *bhikkhus*, would you point to another teacher?”
mn38:24.12: “If you knew and saw in this way, *bhikkhus*, would you return to taking as essential those various observances, agitations, and auspicious rites of the many ascetics and brahmins?”
mn38:24.14: “Is it not the case, *bhikkhus*, that you speak only of what you yourselves have directly known, directly seen, directly understood?”
mn38:24.2: ‘Am I? Am I not? What am I? How am I? Where has this being come from? Where will it go?’”
mn38:24.4: “If you knew and saw in this way, *bhikkhus*, would you say:
mn38:24.5: ‘Our teacher is worthy of respect, and out of respect for our teacher we say this’?”
mn38:24.7: “If you knew and saw in this way, *bhikkhus*, would you say:
mn38:24.8: ‘The ascetic says so, and as ascetics we say so’?”
mn38:25.1: “Sādhu, *bhikkhus*, you have been led by me, *bhikkhus*, by this *dhamma* that is visible here and now, timeless, inviting inspection, leading onward, to be known individually by the wise.
mn38:25.2: This *dhamma*, *bhikkhus*, is visible here and now, timeless, inviting inspection, leading onward, to be known individually by the wise—
mn38:25.3: that is what was said, and it was said in dependence on this.
mn38:26.1: “But, *bhikkhus*, the descent of an embryo takes place through the coming together of three things.
mn38:26.2: Here, the mother and father come together, but the mother is not in season and a gandhabba is not present; in that case there is still no descent of an embryo.
mn38:26.3: Here, the mother and father come together, and the mother is in season, but a gandhabba is not present; in that case there is still no descent of an embryo.
mn38:26.4: But when, *bhikkhus*, the mother and father come together, the mother is in season, and a gandhabba is present—through the coming together of these three things there is the descent of an embryo.
mn38:27.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, the mother carries that embryo in her womb for nine or ten months, with great anxiety, as a heavy burden.
mn38:27.2: Then, *bhikkhus*, after nine or ten months have passed, the mother gives birth to it, with great anxiety, as a heavy burden.
mn38:27.3: When it is born, she nourishes it with her own blood.
mn38:27.4: For that, *bhikkhus*, in the noble one’s *vinaya*, is what is called a mother’s milk.
mn38:28.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, when that boy grows up and his faculties mature,
mn38:28.2: he plays the kinds of children’s games there are, that is: vaṅkaka, ghaṭika, mokkhacika, ciṅgulaka, pattāḷhaka, rathaka, and dhanuka.
mn38:29.1: Then, *bhikkhus*, when that boy grows up and his faculties mature,
mn38:29.2: he amuses himself, furnished and provided with the five cords of sensual pleasure—
mn38:29.3: with forms cognizable by the eye, desirable, lovely, agreeable, pleasing, connected with sensuality, enticing;
mn38:29.4: with sounds cognizable by the ear …
mn38:29.5: with smells cognizable by the nose …
mn38:29.6: with tastes cognizable by the tongue …
mn38:29.7: with tangibles cognizable by the body, desirable, lovely, agreeable, pleasing, connected with sensuality, enticing.
mn38:3.10: In many ways, friend Sāti, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of by the *Bhagavā* as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition, there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn38:3.11: But even though the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, was being questioned, pressed, and admonished by those *bhikkhus*, he still kept insisting on that same evil mistaken view, grasping it stubbornly and clinging to it:
mn38:3.12: “Just so, friends, I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.”
mn38:3.2: “It seems that in the *bhikkhu* named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this evil mistaken view has arisen:
mn38:3.3: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.’”
mn38:3.4: Then those *bhikkhus* went to the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son. Having approached him, they said to him:
mn38:3.5: “Is it true, friend Sāti, that this evil mistaken view has arisen in you:
mn38:3.6: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another’?”
mn38:3.7: “Just so, friends, I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.”
mn38:3.8: Then those *bhikkhus*, wanting to draw the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this evil mistaken view, questioned him, pressed him, and admonished him:
mn38:3.9: “Do not say so, friend Sāti. Do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, for misrepresenting the *Bhagavā* is not good; and the *Bhagavā* would not say that.
mn38:30.1: Having seen a form with the eye, he lusts after a pleasing form and is repelled by a displeasing form, and he dwells with *sati* of the body unestablished [*anupaṭṭhitakāyasati*] and with a constricted mind [*parittacetaso*].
mn38:30.10: having felt a tangible with the body …
mn38:30.11: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, he lusts after a pleasing *dhamma* and is repelled by a displeasing *dhamma*, and he dwells with *sati* of the body unestablished [*anupaṭṭhitakāyasati*] and with a constricted mind [*parittacetaso*].
mn38:30.12: And he does not understand as it truly is that liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*], that liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder.
mn38:30.13: Having thus entered into attraction and aversion, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it.
mn38:30.14: As he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it, delight arises in him.
mn38:30.15: Whatever delight there is in *vedanās*, that is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*]. With clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] as condition there is *bhava*; with *bhava* as condition, birth; with birth as condition, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress come to be.
mn38:30.16: Thus there is the arising of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:30.2: And he does not understand as it truly is that liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*], that liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder.
mn38:30.3: Having thus entered into attraction and aversion, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it.
mn38:30.4: As he delights in that *vedanā*, welcomes it, and remains holding to it, delight arises in him.
mn38:30.5: Whatever delight there is in *vedanās*, that is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*]. With clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] as condition there is *bhava*; with *bhava* as condition, birth; with birth as condition, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress come to be.
mn38:30.6: Thus there is the arising of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:30.7: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn38:30.8: having smelled a smell with the nose …
mn38:30.9: having tasted a taste with the tongue …
mn38:31.1: “Here, *bhikkhus*, a Tathāgata arises in the world, an *arahant*, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, fortunate, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of persons to be tamed, teacher of devatās and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.
mn38:31.2: He declares this world—with its devatās, with its Māras, with its Brahmās, this generation with its ascetics and brahmins, with its devas and humans—having realized it for himself with direct knowledge.
mn38:31.3: He teaches the *dhamma* good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, with meaning and with phrasing;
mn38:31.4: he makes known a wholly complete and pure holy life.
mn38:32.1: That *dhamma* is heard by a householder, or a householder’s son, or someone born in some other family.
mn38:32.2: Having heard that *dhamma*, he gains faith in the Tathāgata.
mn38:32.3: Endowed with that gaining of faith, he reflects thus:
mn38:32.4: ‘The household life is cramped, a dusty path; going forth is open space.
mn38:32.5: It is not easy, while living in a home, to lead the holy life utterly full, utterly pure, polished like a shell.
mn38:32.6: Suppose I were to shave off hair and beard, put on ochre robes, and go forth from the household life into homelessness.’
mn38:32.7: After some time, abandoning a small mass of wealth or a great mass of wealth, abandoning a small circle of relatives or a great circle of relatives, he shaves off hair and beard, puts on ochre robes, and goes forth from the household life into homelessness.
mn38:33.1: Having thus gone forth, he undertakes the training and way of life of *bhikkhus*: abandoning the killing of living beings, he abstains from killing living beings; with rod laid down, with weapon laid down, conscientious, compassionate, he dwells caring for the welfare of all living beings.
mn38:33.10: He abstains from dancing, singing, music, and unsuitable shows,
mn38:33.11: he abstains from garlands, perfumes, ointments, adornments, and embellishments,
mn38:33.12: he abstains from high and luxurious beds,
mn38:33.13: he abstains from accepting gold and silver,
mn38:33.14: he abstains from accepting raw grain,
mn38:33.15: he abstains from accepting raw meat,
mn38:33.16: he abstains from accepting women and girls,
mn38:33.17: he abstains from accepting male and female slaves,
mn38:33.18: he abstains from accepting goats and sheep,
mn38:33.19: he abstains from accepting fowl and pigs,
mn38:33.2: Abandoning the taking of what is not given, he abstains from taking what is not given; taking only what is given, expecting only what is given, he dwells in purity, not stealing.
mn38:33.20: he abstains from accepting elephants, cattle, horses, and mares,
mn38:33.21: he abstains from accepting fields and land,
mn38:33.22: he abstains from running messages and errands,
mn38:33.23: he abstains from buying and selling,
mn38:33.24: he abstains from false weights, false metals, and false measures,
mn38:33.25: he abstains from cheating, deceiving, fraud, and trickery,
mn38:33.26: he abstains from wounding, killing, binding, robbery, plundering, and violence.
mn38:33.3: Abandoning the uncelibate life, he lives the celibate life, living apart, abstaining from the village practice of sexual intercourse.
mn38:33.4: Abandoning false speech, he abstains from false speech; he speaks truth, is devoted to truth, reliable, trustworthy, no deceiver of the world.
mn38:33.5: Abandoning divisive speech, he abstains from divisive speech—what he has heard here he does not tell there to divide those people from these, and what he has heard there he does not tell here to divide these people from those. Thus he is a reconciler of the divided, an encourager of the united, one who delights in concord, rejoices in concord, enjoys concord, and he speaks words that make for concord.
mn38:33.6: Abandoning harsh speech, he abstains from harsh speech—he speaks words that are gentle, pleasing to the ear, lovable, going to the heart, courteous, desired by many, agreeable to many.
mn38:33.7: Abandoning idle chatter, he abstains from idle chatter; he speaks at the proper time, speaks what is factual, what is beneficial, speaks *dhamma*, speaks *vinaya*; at the proper time he speaks words worth treasuring, timely, well-grounded, measured, and connected with benefit.
mn38:33.8: He abstains from injuring seed and plant life,
mn38:33.9: he eats once a day, refraining at night, abstaining from eating at the wrong time.
mn38:34.1: He is content with robes to protect the body and almsfood to sustain the belly. Wherever he goes, he goes taking only these with him.
mn38:34.2: Just as a bird, wherever it flies, flies with only the burden of its wings;
mn38:34.3: so too a *bhikkhu* is content with robes to protect the body and almsfood to sustain the belly. Wherever he goes, he goes taking only these with him.
mn38:34.4: Endowed with this noble aggregate of virtue, he experiences within himself a blameless sukha.
mn38:35.1: Having seen a form with the eye, he does not grasp at its signs or features.
mn38:35.2: Since, if he were to dwell with the faculty of the eye unguarded, evil unskillful *dhammas* of longing and dejection might invade him, he practices for its restraint, guards the faculty of the eye, and undertakes restraint of the faculty of the eye.
mn38:35.3: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn38:35.4: having smelled a smell with the nose …
mn38:35.5: having tasted a taste with the tongue …
mn38:35.6: having felt a tangible with the body …
mn38:35.7: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, he does not grasp at its signs or features.
mn38:35.8: Since, if he were to dwell with the faculty of the mind unguarded, evil unskillful *dhammas* of longing and dejection might invade him, he practices for its restraint, guards the faculty of the mind, and undertakes restraint of the faculty of the mind.
mn38:35.9: Endowed with this noble restraint of the faculties, he experiences within himself an unblemished sukha.
mn38:36.1: He acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when going forward and returning; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when looking ahead and looking away; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] when bending and stretching; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in wearing his outer robe, bowl, and robes; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in defecating and urinating; he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*] in walking, standing, sitting, sleeping, waking, speaking, and keeping silent.
mn38:37.1: Endowed with this noble aggregate of virtue, with this noble contentment, with this noble restraint of the faculties, and with this noble *sati* and full awareness [*satisampajaññena*],
mn38:37.2: he resorts to a secluded resting place—a forest, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a hillside cave, a charnel ground, a jungle grove, the open air, a heap of straw.
mn38:38.1: After the meal, on returning from almsround, he sits down cross-legged, setting his body erect, and establishing *sati* in front of him.
mn38:38.2: Abandoning longing for the world, he dwells with a mind [*citta*] free from longing, and he purifies the mind [*citta*] from longing;
mn38:38.3: abandoning ill will and malice, he dwells with a mind [*citta*] free from ill will, caring for the welfare of all living beings, and he purifies the mind [*citta*] from ill will and malice;
mn38:38.4: abandoning dullness and drowsiness, he dwells free from dullness and drowsiness, perceiving light, with *sati* and full awareness [*sampajāno*], and he purifies the mind [*citta*] from dullness and drowsiness;
mn38:38.5: abandoning restlessness and remorse, he dwells unagitated, with his mind [*citta*] inwardly settled, and he purifies the mind [*citta*] from restlessness and remorse;
mn38:38.6: abandoning doubt, he dwells having gone beyond doubt, free from uncertainty about skillful *dhammas*, and he purifies the mind [*citta*] from doubt.
mn38:39.1: Having abandoned these five hindrances, defilements of the mind [*citta*] that weaken discernment [*paññāya*],
mn38:39.2: quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which is accompanied by thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with pīti and sukha born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn38:39.3: Furthermore, *bhikkhus*, with the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, which has internal confidence and unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of the mind [*citta*], without thought [*avitakkaṁ*] and without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with pīti and sukha born of *samādhi* …
mn38:39.5: he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn38:4.1: Since those *bhikkhus* could not draw the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this evil mistaken view, they went to the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, they paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, they said to the *Bhagavā*:
mn38:4.10: ‘Just so, friends, I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.’
mn38:4.11: Then, *bhante*, wanting to draw the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this evil mistaken view, we questioned him, pressed him, and admonished him:
mn38:4.12: ‘Do not say so, friend Sāti. Do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, for misrepresenting the *Bhagavā* is not good; and the *Bhagavā* would not say that.
mn38:4.13: In many ways, friend Sāti, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of by the *Bhagavā* as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition, there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].’
mn38:4.14: But even though the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, was being questioned, pressed, and admonished by us, *bhante*, he still kept insisting on that same evil mistaken view, grasping it stubbornly and clinging to it:
mn38:4.15: ‘Just so, friends, I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.’
mn38:4.16: Since we could not draw the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, away from this evil mistaken view, *bhante*, we have reported this matter to the *Bhagavā*.”
mn38:4.2: “*Bhante*, in the *bhikkhu* named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this evil mistaken view has arisen:
mn38:4.3: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.’
mn38:4.4: We heard, *bhante*, that in the *bhikkhu* named Sāti, the fisherman’s son, this evil mistaken view had arisen:
mn38:4.5: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.’
mn38:4.6: Then we went to the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son. Having approached him, we said:
mn38:4.7: ‘Is it true, friend Sāti, that this evil mistaken view has arisen in you:
mn38:4.8: “As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another”?’
mn38:4.9: When this was said, *bhante*, the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, said to us:
mn38:40.1: Having seen a form with the eye, he does not lust after a pleasing form and is not repelled by a displeasing form; he dwells with *sati* of the body established [*upaṭṭhitakāyasati*] and with a measureless mind [*appamāṇacetaso*].
mn38:40.2: And he understands as it truly is that liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*], that liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder.
mn38:40.3: Thus, having abandoned attraction and aversion, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it.
mn38:40.4: As he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it, whatever delight there is in *vedanās* ceases.
mn38:40.5: With the cessation of delight, there is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*]; with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], the cessation of *bhava*; with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth; with the cessation of birth, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress cease.
mn38:40.6: Thus there is the cessation of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:41.1: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn38:41.10: Thus there is the cessation of this whole mass of dukkha.
mn38:41.11: You should remember this from me, *bhikkhus*, as a brief exposition of liberation through the ending of *taṇhā*; but the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, as one ensnared in the great net and mass of *taṇhā*.”
mn38:41.13: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn38:41.14: The Greater Discourse on the Ending of *taṇhā* is finished, the eighth.
mn38:41.2: having smelled a smell with the nose …
mn38:41.3: having tasted a taste with the tongue …
mn38:41.4: having felt a tangible with the body …
mn38:41.5: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, he does not lust after a pleasing *dhamma* and is not repelled by a displeasing *dhamma*; he dwells with *sati* of the body established [*upaṭṭhitakāyasati*] and with a measureless mind [*appamāṇacetaso*],
mn38:41.6: and he understands as it truly is that liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*], that liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], where those evil unskillful *dhammas* cease without remainder.
mn38:41.7: Thus, having abandoned attraction and aversion, whatever *vedanā* he feels—pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant—he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it.
mn38:41.8: As he does not delight in that *vedanā*, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it, whatever delight there is in *vedanās* ceases.
mn38:41.9: With the cessation of delight, there is the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*]; with the cessation of clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*], the cessation of *bhava*; with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth; with the cessation of birth, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, dukkha, dejection, and distress cease.
mn38:5.11: “It is that, *bhante*, which speaks and feels, and which experiences here and there the result of good and bad kammas.”
mn38:5.12: “Foolish man, whom have you ever known me to teach the *dhamma* in that way?
mn38:5.13: Have I not, foolish man, in many ways spoken of consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*], saying that apart from a condition there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*]?
mn38:5.14: And yet you, foolish man, through your own bad grasp, both misrepresent us and injure yourself, and you generate much demerit.
mn38:5.15: For that will lead to your harm and suffering for a long time, foolish man.”
mn38:5.2: “Come, *bhikkhu*, in my name address the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son:
mn38:5.4: “Yes, *bhante*,” that *bhikkhu* replied to the *Bhagavā*. Then he went to the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, and said to him:
mn38:5.6: “Yes, friend,” the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, replied to that *bhikkhu*. Then he went to the *Bhagavā*, paid homage to the *Bhagavā*, and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the *Bhagavā* said to the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son:
mn38:5.7: “Is it true, Sāti, that this evil mistaken view has arisen in you:
mn38:5.8: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another’?”
mn38:5.9: “Just so, *bhante*, I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: it is this very consciousness [*viññāṇa*] that runs on and wanders through *saṃsāra*, and not another.”
mn38:6.3: is this *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, even warmed up in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*]?”
mn38:6.6: When this was said, the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, sat silent, dismayed, with drooping shoulders, downcast, brooding, at a loss for words.
mn38:6.7: Then the *Bhagavā*, knowing that the *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, was silent, dismayed, with drooping shoulders, downcast, brooding, and at a loss for words, said to him:
mn38:6.8: “You will be exposed, foolish man, by this evil mistaken view of your own.
mn38:6.9: Now I will question the *bhikkhus* here.”
mn38:7.2: “Do you too understand the *dhamma* taught by me in the way that this *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, through his own bad grasp both misrepresents us and injures himself and generates much demerit?”
mn38:7.4: For in many ways, *bhante*, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of by the *Bhagavā* as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition, there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn38:7.6: It is good that you understand the *dhamma* taught by me in this way, *bhikkhus*.
mn38:7.7: For in many ways, *bhikkhus*, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] has been spoken of by me as *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannaṁ*]; apart from a condition, there is no arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].”
mn38:7.8: Yet this *bhikkhu* Sāti, the fisherman’s son, through his own bad grasp both misrepresents us and injures himself and generates much demerit.
mn38:7.9: For that will lead to the harm and suffering of that foolish man for a long time.
mn38:8.1: “Whatever condition, *bhikkhus*, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises dependent on, by just that it is reckoned.
mn38:8.10: dependent on splinters, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a splinter fire;
mn38:8.11: dependent on grass, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a grass fire;
mn38:8.12: dependent on cow dung, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a cow-dung fire;
mn38:8.13: dependent on chaff, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a chaff fire;
mn38:8.14: dependent on rubbish, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a rubbish fire.
mn38:8.15: So too, *bhikkhus*, whatever condition consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises dependent on, by just that it is reckoned.
mn38:8.16: Dependent on the eye and forms, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.17: dependent on the ear and sounds, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.18: dependent on the nose and smells, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.19: dependent on the tongue and tastes, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇantveva*].
mn38:8.2: Dependent on the eye and forms, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.20: Dependent on the body and tangibles, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇantveva*].
mn38:8.21: Dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇantveva*].
mn38:8.3: dependent on the ear and sounds, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.4: dependent on the nose and smells, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.5: dependent on the tongue and tastes, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.6: dependent on the body and tangibles, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇantveva*];
mn38:8.7: dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] arises; it is reckoned simply as mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇantveva*].
mn38:8.8: Just as, *bhikkhus*, whatever condition a fire burns dependent on, by just that it is reckoned.
mn38:8.9: Dependent on wood, a fire burns; it is reckoned simply as a wood fire;
mn38:9.1: “Do you see, *bhikkhus*: ‘This has come to be’?”
mn38:9.3: “Do you see: ‘This has arisen from nutriment’?”
mn38:9.5: “Do you see, *bhikkhus*, that with the cessation of that nutriment, what has come to be is of a nature to cease?”
